OBJECTION HEARING CALENDAR
Off Calendar Aug-15-2018 Off Calendar

Defendants Roman Shtylman And Bitfloor, Inc.'S Motion For Relief From Order Pursuant To C.C.P. Section 473
Off Calendar Aug-10-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Kyle Z. Pineo of the law offices of Berding & Weil LLP (925-838-2090) appears by telephone as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorneys, Joseph Gallagher of the law offices of Harris St. Laurent & Chaudhry LLP (917-512-9464) and Teresa L. Juggins of the law offices of Baker Marquart LLP (424-652-7813) appear as counsels for defendants/moving parties

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter. The matter is first taken UNDER SUBMISSION and later ruled upon. The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

Defendants Roman Shtylman And Bitfloor, Inc.'S Motion For Relief From Order Pursuant To C.C.P. Section 473 is DENIED. Back in June, I overruled defendants' demurrer asserting lack of personal jurisdiction, because it was contrary to California law. "Lack of personal jurisdiction over defendant is not ground for demurrer. It must be raised, if at all, by motion to quash service of summons [CCP 418.10'] If defendant instead demurs, the demurrer constitutes a general appearance, waiving any defect in personal jurisdiction [CCP 1014]". (Weil & Brown, California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (TRG 2016) p. 7(I)-36 (emphasis in original); see also id at p. 3-106.) Defendants now move for relief under Code of Civil Procedure 473, conceding their "mistake," but claiming it was "excusable." However, defendants also concede that whether a mistake is excusable is judged by whether a "reasonably prudent" attorney would have made it. (See Zamora v. Clayborn Contracting Group, Inc. (2002) 28 Cal.4th 249, 258.) Reasonably prudent California litigation lawyers know the requirements for challenging personal jurisdiction. Even attorneys who do not know the rules can ascertain them in a matter of seconds - e.g., by consulting the Weil & Brown treatise. Moreover, defendants cite no authority holding that, once waived, a lack of personal jurisdiction claim can be revived. Defendants say their New York lawyers drafted the demurrers, but pro hac vice counsel are subject to the same professional standards as California counsel. (See Weil & Brown, p. 9(I)-168; CRC 9.40.) Written order is signed, filed and electronically served.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, e-mail: torreanom@aol.org (L&M302)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jul-18-2018 Set For Trial On Feb-04-19 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

OBJECTION HEARING CALENDAR
Off Calendar Aug-02-2018 Reset From Aug-01-18 To Aug-15-18 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-27-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Atty. Kyle Z. Pineo of Berding/Weil, Tel. No. 1-925-838-2090 appearing
for the Plaintiff;
Atty. Ethan A. Balogh of Coleman & Balogh LLP, Tel. No. 1-415-391-0440 specially
appearing for the Defendants.

RULING - A R G U E D; DEFENDANTS ROMAN SHTYLMAN AND BITFLOOR, INC.'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT is taken under submission. Judge: Richard Ulmer; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Robert Balian, CSR #5220 =32/ru

* * * After hearing, the court signed an order and ruled as follows: DEFENDANTS ROMAN SHTYLMAN and BITFLOOR, INC.'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT - Lack of personal jurisdiction over a defendant is not a ground for demurrer. It must be raised, if at all, by a motion to quash service of summons. (CCP 418.10; Weil & Brown, California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (TRG 2016) 7:65.) Defendants' reply brief asserts that jurisdiction is lacking over the Poe plaintiffs, but this is not a ground stated in defendants' demurrers. Plaintiffs adequately plead alter ego and veil piercing theories. Defendants say these claims will not be proven, but such argument is not for the pleading stage. Under these theories, a court may disregard the corporate entity and treat the acts as if they were done by the individuals. (See Shapoff v. Scull (1990) 222 Cal.App.3d 1457, 1466, 1469.) As to the individual causes of action, defendants misapprehend California pleading law. "To survive a demurrer, the complaint need only alleged facts sufficient to state a cause of action; each evidentiary fact that might eventually form part of the plaintiff's proof need not be alleged." (C.A. v. William S. Hart Union High School Dist. (2012) 53 Cal.4th 861, 872.) Moreover, where, as here, all the demurrers are general demurrers under Code of Civil Procedure 430.10(e): "Objections that a complaint is ambiguous or uncertain, or that essential facts appear only inferentially, or as conclusions of law, or by way of recitals" may not be raised. (Johnson v. Mead (1987) 191 Cal.App.3d 156,160.) 1st & 5th (Conversion; Negligence): The claims are adequately pled under California law. Gomes v. Countrywide Hone Loans, Inc. (2011) 192 Cal.App.4th 1149 does not, as defendants assert, hold that "the source" of an allegation must be pled.
2nd (Breach of Contract): The claim is adequately pled, and Shtylman may be liable under the complaint's alter ego theory. 3rd & 4th (Open Book Account; Account Stated): The claims are adequately pled. It does not matter that the "book" and/or "account" may be electronic.
6th & 7th (Fraud; Fraudulent Transfer): The claims are pled with sufficient specificity.
8th (Conspiracy): The claim is adequately pled. More than a conspiracy between Shtylman and Bitfloor is alleged, and defendants may be liable for conspiracy with third parties.
9th (Breach of Fiduciary Duty): The claim is adequately pled, and Shytlman may be liable under the complaint's alter ego theory. Defendants' demurrers are OVERRULED in their entirety. * * *
